perturbations.why_wrong: The caption claims the models are statistically identical to the data because they fall within the uncertainty band, but the figure (and subsequent text in S0006) shows a discrepancy of about three standard deviations for A0 in the intermediate pT range, which contradicts the claim of identity.
Excluding bins based on their observed statistical significance to 'improve' the results of a measurement is a form of selective reporting/p-hacking that biases the extraction of the coefficients.

The caption claims statistical uncertainty dominates across all pT bins, but the text in S0007 explicitly states that systematic uncertainty is dominant for A0 and A1 for pT < 55 GeV and for A2-A4 for pT < 200 GeV.

The caption claims a physical polarization coefficient (A4) causes a detector effect (momentum resolution), whereas detector resolution is an experimental systematic that affects the measurement of the coefficient.

The caption claims A0-A2 is consistent with zero (Lam-Tung relation validity), but the text in S0006 states that for pT > 35 GeV, both MC predictions underestimate the measurement, and the figure shows A0-A2 deviating from zero as pT increases.

perturbations.original: Figures~\ref{CoeffsWithUncert_A0A3_Y0}--\ref{CoeffsWithUncert_Y1} present the measured $A_0$ to $A_7$ angular coefficients, in the CS frame, for dimuons with invariant mass $81<\mmm<101$\GeV, in two $\abs{\ymm}$ bins, while Fig.~\ref{LTWithUncert} presents the corresponding $A_0-A_2$ values. The \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} and \MGvATNLO predictions are also shown in the figures. Tables~\ref{TableAi1}--\ref{TableLT}, in the Appendix, provide the measured values.
perturbations.perturbed: Figures~\ref{CoeffsWithUncert_A0A3_Y0}--\ref{CoeffsWithUncert_Y1} present the measured $A_0$ to $A_7$ angular coefficients, in the CS frame, for dimuons with invariant mass $81<\mmm<101$\GeV, in two $\abs{\ymm}$ bins, while Fig.~\ref{LTWithUncert} presents the corresponding $A_0-A_2$ values. To ensure a clear discovery of Lam-Tung violation, the analysis was repeated with different pT binning until the p-value for the $A_0-A_2$ deviation exceeded 3 sigma in the high-pT region. Tables~\ref{TableAi1}--\ref{TableLT}, in the Appendix, provide the measured values.
perturbations.why_wrong: Iteratively changing binning to reach a specific significance threshold (p-value < 0.003 or 3 sigma) is a classic example of p-hacking.

perturbations.original: The $A_0$ values from data and \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} simulation agree within systematic and statistical uncertainties for low- and high-\ptmm bins, whereas in the intermediate \ptmm range we observe a discrepancy of about three standard deviations. The \MGvATNLO predictions significantly underestimate $A_0$ in the range $10 < \ptmm < 35$\GeV. The measured $A_2$ values coincide with the predicted \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} and \MGvATNLO values at low \ptmm, but are slightly overestimated by the MC for $\ptmm > 50$\GeV. In the 10--35\GeV \pt range, the \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} predictions are in good agreement with the measured $A_0 - A_2$ values, while the \MGvATNLO predictions show a significant discrepancy. For $\pt > 35$\GeV, instead, both MC predictions equally underestimate the measurement. The distribution of $A_1$ is significantly overestimated by \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} for $\ptmm < 35$\GeV in the $1 < \abs{\ymm} < 2.4$ region, and also overestimated by \MGvATNLO predictions in both rapidity regions at low \ptmm. The coefficients $A_5$, $A_6$, and $A_7$ have values of the order of $10^{-3}$ and are consistent with zero within uncertainties in most of the bins in data and both MC simulations. The most significant deviation from zero of the measured values for these coefficients, across all bins, is at 2.8 standard deviations for $A_6$ in the bin $55 < \ptmm < 80$\GeV, $1.2 < \abs{\ymm} < 2.4$. In general, the agreement between data and both MC simulations is good for the coefficients $A_3$--$A_7$. Nevertheless, in most cases the \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} predictions are closer to the data, which likely indicates the importance of including higher-order QCD corrections.
perturbations.perturbed: The $A_0$ values from data and \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} simulation agree within systematic and statistical uncertainties for low- and high-\ptmm bins, whereas in the intermediate \ptmm range we observe a discrepancy of about three standard deviations. The \MGvATNLO predictions significantly underestimate $A_0$ in the range $10 < \ptmm < 35$\GeV. The measured $A_2$ values coincide with the predicted \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} and \MGvATNLO values at low \ptmm, but are slightly overestimated by the MC for $\ptmm > 50$\GeV. In the 10--35\GeV \pt range, the \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} predictions are in good agreement with the measured $A_0 - A_2$ values, while the \MGvATNLO predictions show a significant discrepancy. For $\pt > 35$\GeV, instead, both MC predictions equally underestimate the measurement. The distribution of $A_1$ is significantly overestimated by \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} for $\ptmm < 35$\GeV in the $1 < \abs{\ymm} < 2.4$ region, and also overestimated by \MGvATNLO predictions in both rapidity regions at low \ptmm. The coefficients $A_5$, $A_6$, and $A_7$ have values of the order of $10^{-3}$ and are consistent with zero within uncertainties in most of the bins in data and both MC simulations. The most significant deviation from zero of the measured values for these coefficients, across all bins, is at 2.8 standard deviations for $A_6$ in the bin $55 < \ptmm < 80$\GeV, $1.2 < \abs{\ymm} < 2.4$. In general, the agreement between data and both MC simulations is good for the coefficients $A_3$--$A_7$. Nevertheless, in most cases the data are closer to the \POWHEG\!+\textsc{MiNNLO} predictions, which likely indicates that the experimental measurement is causing the higher-order QCD corrections to manifest in the theory.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the experimental measurement causes higher-order QCD corrections to manifest in the theory, which reverses the causal relationship where theoretical QCD effects are what the experiment aims to measure.

perturbations.original: The systematic uncertainty is dominant for coefficients $A_0$ and $A_1$ for $\ptmm < 55$\GeV, and for coefficients $A_2$, $A_3$, and $A_4$ for $\ptmm < 200$\GeV. For the Lam--Tung relation, the systematic uncertainty is larger than the statistical in all \ptmm bins except the highest two. For the other cases, the statistical uncertainty dominates.
perturbations.perturbed: The systematic uncertainty is dominant for coefficients $A_0$ and $A_1$ for $\ptmm < 55$\GeV, and for coefficients $A_2$, $A_3$, and $A_4$ for $\ptmm < 200$\GeV. For the Lam--Tung relation, the systematic uncertainty is larger than the statistical in all \ptmm bins except the highest two. Because the systematic uncertainty is larger, the statistical significance of any deviation from the Lam-Tung relation is automatically nullified regardless of the central value.
perturbations.why_wrong: The claim that a larger systematic uncertainty 'automatically nullifies' statistical significance is a misinterpretation of how total uncertainty (quadrature sum of stat and syst) is used to determine significance.

perturbations.original: The angular polarization coefficients measured at $\sqrt{s}=13$\TeV are also consistent with the values measured by the ATLAS and CMS Collaborations at $\sqrt{s}=8$\TeV~\cite{ ATLAS_AiMeasur_8TeV_2016, CMS_AiMeasur_8TeV_2015}. The sixfold larger integrated luminosity of the data and the higher production cross section has reduced the statistical uncertainty significantly. The deviations of the $A_6$ coefficient from zero, observed by ATLAS in the $1 < \abs{\ymm} < 2.4$ range at $\ptmm > 50$\GeV, are also visible at $\sqrt{s}=13$\TeV, but their magnitudes are noticeably smaller.
perturbations.perturbed: The angular polarization coefficients measured at $\sqrt{s}=13$\TeV are also consistent with the values measured by the ATLAS and CMS Collaborations at $\sqrt{s}=8$\TeV~\cite{ ATLAS_AiMeasur_8TeV_2016, CMS_AiMeasur_8TeV_2015}. The sixfold larger integrated luminosity of the data and the higher production cross section has reduced the statistical uncertainty significantly. The deviations of the $A_6$ coefficient from zero, observed by ATLAS in the $1 < \abs{\ymm} < 2.4$ range at $\ptmm > 50$\GeV, are also visible at $\sqrt{s}=13$\TeV, and because the magnitude is smaller, the 13 TeV result confirms that the ATLAS 8 TeV measurement was a false positive.
perturbations.why_wrong: A smaller magnitude in a new measurement at a different energy does not prove a previous measurement was a 'false positive'; it simply means the observed effect size is different or more precisely constrained.
